content:
# 2 Thessalonians Chapter 3 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus, PDF page 205 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/2TH_003_page_001.jpg

---

**1** Finally, brothers and sisters, pray for us — that the Lord's message will spread rapidly and be honored everywhere, just as it was among you,

**2** and that we will be rescued from warped and wicked people, for not everyone has faith.

**3** But the Lord is faithful, and he will strengthen you and guard you from the evil one.

**4** We have confidence in the Lord about you — that you are doing, and will continue to do, what we instruct.

**5** May the Lord direct your hearts toward the love of God and the steadfastness of Christ.

**6** Now we command you, brothers and sisters, in the name of our Lord Jesus Christ: keep away from every believer who lives an undisciplined life and does not follow the tradition you received from us.

**7** For you yourselves know how you ought to follow our example. We were not undisciplined among you,

**8** and we never ate anyone's food without paying for it. Instead, we worked hard, laboring night and day, so as not to be a burden to any of you.

**9** It was not that we lacked the right to be supported — we did it to give you a pattern to follow.

**10** In fact, when we were with you, we gave you this rule: "Anyone who refuses to work should not eat."

**11** We say this because we hear that some among you are living undisciplined lives — not keeping busy with work, but being busybodies.

**12** People like that we command and urge, through the Lord Jesus Christ, to settle down, do their own work, and earn the food they eat.

**13** As for the rest of you, brothers and sisters, never get tired of doing what is right.

**14** If anyone does not obey what we say in this letter, take note of that person and do not associate with them, so that they will feel ashamed.

**15** Yet do not treat them as an enemy — warn them as you would a brother or sister.

**16** Now may the Lord of peace himself give you peace at all times and in every way. The Lord be with all of you.

**17** This greeting is in my own hand — Paul. This is my signature in every letter; this is how I write.

**18** The grace of our Lord Jesus Christ be with all of you.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**Verse 2 — "warped and wicked people" (ΑΤΟΠΩΝ ΚΑΙ ΠΟΝΗΡΩΝ ΑΝΘΡΩΠΩΝ):** ΑΤΟΠΟΣ literally means "out of place" — something that does not fit where it belongs. Many translations use "perverse" or "unreasonable." I chose "warped" because it captures the sense of people whose thinking has been bent out of proper shape, distinguishing it from ΠΟΝΗΡΩΝ (actively wicked). Together the pair conveys both distorted character and malicious intent.

**Verse 3 — "the evil one" (ΤΟΥ ΠΟΝΗΡΟΥ):** The Greek is ambiguous — ΠΟΝΗΡΟΥ could be neuter ("evil" as an abstract force) or masculine ("the evil one" as a personal being). Given Paul's contrast between the faithfulness of the Lord and a singular opposing threat, I rendered it as personal. The Sinaiticus text supports either reading.

**Verse 5 — "the steadfastness of Christ" (ΥΠΟΜΟΝΗΝ ΤΟΥ ΧΡΙΣΤΟΥ):** This phrase can mean either "the patience that Christ himself showed" or "the endurance that comes from waiting for Christ." The Sinaiticus preserves the genitive without clarification. I used "steadfastness of Christ," which holds both meanings — Christ's own patient endurance and the perseverance that belongs to those who follow him.

**Verse 6 — "undisciplined" (ΑΤΑΚΤΩΣ):** This is the same word family discussed in 1 Thessalonians 5:14 (ΑΤΑΚΤΟΥΣ). Originally a military term for a soldier who breaks rank, it had evolved by Paul's time to mean general disorderliness. In context, Paul means specifically those who have stopped working — likely because they believed Christ's return was so imminent that normal life was pointless. I used "undisciplined" rather than "idle" here because Paul's concern extends beyond mere laziness to a deliberate rejection of communal expectations.

**Verse 8 — "never ate anyone's food without paying for it" (ΔΩΡΕΑΝ ΑΡΤΟΝ ΕΦΑΓΟΜΕΝ):** Literally "we did not eat bread freely/as a gift." The point is not about restaurant bills but about apostolic independence — Paul and his companions refused to be financially supported by the community even though they had every right to be (v. 9). I paraphrased to make the economic principle clear to modern readers.

**Verse 11 — "not keeping busy with work, but being busybodies" (ΜΗΔΕΝ ΕΡΓΑΖΟΜΕΝΟΥΣ ΑΛΛΑ ΠΕΡΙΕΡΓΑΖΟΜΕΝΟΥΣ):** Paul delivers a sharp wordplay in Greek: ΕΡΓΑΖΟΜΕΝΟΥΣ (working) versus ΠΕΡΙΕΡΓΑΖΟΜΕΝΟΥΣ (working around, meddling). I preserved the wordplay with "keeping busy" and "busybodies," which mirrors Paul's rhetorical technique of turning a word against itself.

**Verse 14 — "do not associate with them" (ΜΗ ΣΥΝΑΝΑΜΙΓΝΥΣΘΑΙ ΑΥΤΩ):** The Sinaiticus reads this as a command to withdraw social fellowship. Yet verse 15 immediately softens the instruction — the person is still a brother or sister, not an enemy. Paul walks a careful line between communal discipline and ongoing care. The goal is shame that leads to restoration, not permanent exclusion.

**Verse 17 — "This greeting is in my own hand — Paul":** Paul apparently dictated most of his letters through a scribe (see Romans 16:22, where Tertius identifies himself). Here he takes the pen himself to sign off, establishing the letter's authenticity. This was especially important given concerns about forged letters circulating in Paul's name (see 2:2). The Sinaiticus preserves this personal note clearly at the close of the third column.

— PNEUMA
